fact: Karnataka, a state in South India, was formed on November 1, 1956, and was renamed from 'Mysore State' to 'Karnataka' in 1973.
Hampi, a ruined city in Karnataka and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire and was once among the richest cities in the world during the 14th–16th centuries.
The Gomateshwara statue at Shravanabelagola in Karnataka, standing about 57 feet tall, is carved from a single block of granite and is over 1,000 years old.
The Mysore Dasara, a 10-day festival celebrated in the city of Mysuru, has been observed for over 400 years, dating back to the Vijayanagara Empire.
Karnataka has the second-highest number of nationally protected monuments in India, after Uttar Pradesh.
The Vijayanagara Empire, one of South India's greatest medieval empires, ruled from Hampi (in present-day Karnataka) between the 14th and 16th centuries.
Maski, a town in Karnataka's Raichur district, is where the first Ashoka edict mentioning Emperor Ashoka by name was discovered, confirming his historical identity.
Mysore Pak, a popular Indian sweet made of ghee, sugar, and gram flour, was invented in the kitchens of the Mysore Palace by a royal cook named Kakasura Madappa.
Bisi Bele Bath, a traditional Karnataka rice dish, literally means 'hot lentil rice' in Kannada.
The Masala Dosa, a famous South Indian crepe stuffed with spiced potato, is believed to have originated in Udupi, a coastal town in Karnataka.
Davangere, a city in central Karnataka, is famous for 'Benne Dosa' — a crispy dosa drenched in white butter ('benne' means butter in Kannada).
Coorg (Kodagu), a hilly region in Karnataka, is known for Pandi Curry — a spicy pork dish made with 'kachampuli,' a local vinegar from the garcinia fruit.
Chikmagalur, a district in Karnataka, is the birthplace of Indian coffee — legend says a Sufi saint named Baba Budan smuggled seven coffee beans from Yemen in the 17th century.
Dharwad Pedha, a milk-based sweet from the city of Dharwad in Karnataka, holds a Geographical Indication (GI) tag and has been made by the same family for around 175 years.
The Kannada film industry, based in Karnataka, is nicknamed 'Sandalwood' because of the state's historic association with Mysore sandalwood trade.
Dr. Rajkumar, a legendary Kannada actor, acted in over 200 films and also won a National Award as a playback singer.
The Kannada film 'KGF: Chapter 2' (2022) became one of the highest-grossing Indian films of all time.
'Samskara' (1970), based on a Kannada novel by U.R. Ananthamurthy, won the first National Film Award for Best Feature Film given to a Kannada movie.
Girish Kasaravalli, a Kannada filmmaker, is one of the few Indian directors to win the National Film Award for Best Feature Film four times.
Karnataka has produced eight Jnanpith Award winners (India's highest literary honor) for Kannada literature — the most for any Indian language, tied with Hindi.
Kuvempu, a celebrated Kannada poet and Jnanpith laureate, wrote Karnataka's state anthem 'Jaya Bharata Jananiya Tanujate.'
The Vachana movement of 12th-century Karnataka, led by the saint-poet Basavanna, produced revolutionary poetry that challenged caste and gender hierarchies.
Pampa, known as the 'Adikavi' (first poet) of Kannada literature, wrote his epic 'Vikramarjuna Vijaya' in the 10th century.
Yakshagana, a traditional folk theater form from coastal Karnataka, features elaborate costumes, painted faces, and overnight performances blending dance, music, and dialogue.
Jog Falls, located in Karnataka's Shimoga district, drops about 253 meters (830 feet), making it one of the highest plunge waterfalls in India.
Karnataka has five national parks, including Bandipur and Nagarhole — both major tiger reserves in South India.
Kudremukh, a mountain range in Karnataka, gets its name from the Kannada words meaning 'horse face,' inspired by its distinctive shape.
A large portion of the Western Ghats — a UNESCO biodiversity hotspot running along India's west coast — lies within Karnataka.
Karnataka's coastline along the Arabian Sea stretches roughly 320 km, dotted with beaches and port towns like Mangaluru and Karwar.
Bengaluru, the capital of Karnataka, is called the 'Silicon Valley of India' and hosts the headquarters of Infosys, Wipro, and ISRO.
Channapatna, a town near Bengaluru, is famous for its colorful lacquered wooden toys — a craft that reportedly began during Tipu Sultan's reign and now holds a GI tag.
Mysore Silk sarees and Mysore Sandalwood Soap are both produced by Karnataka state-run enterprises founded by the Wadiyar royal family in the early 1900s.
Bengaluru's name reportedly comes from 'Benda Kaaluru' (Kannada for 'town of boiled beans'), based on a folk legend involving a hungry Hoysala king.
Karnataka's official state animal is the Indian elephant, and its state bird is the Indian roller — a brightly colored bird known for its acrobatic flight.
Kannada, the official language of Karnataka, is one of India's classical languages with a literary history stretching back over 1,500 years.
